---
title: "Distributions"
module: "M2 — Math You Actually Need"
chapter: 10
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M2 Ch9 — Probability basics"
learning_objectives:
  - "Read a distribution as the shape of randomness: which values are common and which are rare"
  - "Tell a uniform distribution (all outcomes equally likely) apart from a bell-shaped normal one (values cluster near the middle)"
  - "See a distribution by drawing many samples with a fixed seed and plotting a histogram"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 16
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: what-is-a-distribution
    q: "What is a distribution?"
    a: "The shape that randomness takes — which values are common and which are rare across all the outcomes a random quantity can produce."
  - id: uniform-vs-normal
    q: "How does a uniform distribution differ from a normal (bell-shaped) one?"
    a: "A uniform distribution makes every outcome equally likely, so its histogram is flat (like a fair die). A normal distribution clusters values near the middle and thins out at the edges, so its histogram is a bell."
  - id: see-by-sampling
    q: "How do you see a distribution in code?"
    a: "Draw many samples from it with a fixed seed for reproducibility, then plot a histogram — the bars approximate the underlying shape."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Roll a fair die many times and write down the results. The 1s, 2s, 3s, 4s, 5s, and
6s come up in roughly equal piles — no number is favoured. Now measure the height of
many people instead. Most cluster near the average, a few are unusually short or
tall, and the extremes are rare. Both are random, but the **shape** of the
randomness is different: the die is *flat*, the heights are a *hump in the middle*.

That shape is a **distribution** — a description of which values are common and
which are rare. It is one of the most useful ideas in this whole course, because the
numbers a model touches (pixel values, word counts, weights, errors) all have a
distribution, and knowing the shape tells you what to expect. In this chapter you
meet two shapes — the flat **uniform** and the bell-shaped **normal** — and you
learn the one move that makes any distribution visible: draw a pile of samples and
plot a **histogram**.

> You'll need from before: probability as the long-run fraction of times an outcome
> happens, between 0 and 1 (M2 Ch9). A distribution is that idea spread across
> *every* possible value at once.

---

## A distribution is the shape of randomness

A random quantity can land on many values. Its **distribution** answers, for the
whole range at once: how likely is each value? Two shapes cover most of what you
need early on.

- **Uniform** — every outcome is equally likely. A fair six-sided die is uniform
  over `1..6`: each face has probability `1/6`. Drawn as a picture, a uniform
  distribution is **flat** — no value is preferred over another.
- **Normal** (also called *Gaussian*, or informally a *bell curve*) — values
  **cluster near a centre** and grow rarer as you move away in either direction.
  Heights, measurement errors, and the average of many small effects tend to look
  normal. Drawn as a picture, it is a symmetric **bell**: tall in the middle,
  thin tails at the edges.

The contrast is the whole lesson. *Uniform* says "anything in range, equally."
*Normal* says "probably near the middle, occasionally far out." Most real-world
measurements are closer to the bell than to the flat line.

### Seeing a shape: draw samples, then a histogram

You rarely get handed a distribution's formula. Instead you get **samples** — actual
draws — and you reconstruct the shape from them. The tool for that is a
**histogram**: chop the value range into equal **bins** (buckets), then count how
many samples fall in each bin and draw a bar of that height. Common values build
tall bars; rare values build short ones. With enough samples, the bars trace the
underlying shape — flat for uniform, a bell for normal.

Two practical notes carry through the rest of the module:

- **More samples, clearer shape.** Ten draws look noisy; a thousand draws settle
  into the true shape. A histogram is an *estimate*, and the estimate sharpens as
  samples grow.
- **Fix the seed for reproducibility.** Random draws differ every run, which makes
  results hard to compare or debug. Seeding the random generator (here,
  `np.random.default_rng(0)`) makes the "random" sequence the same every time, so
  your plot and your numbers are repeatable.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 🧱 For Java developers

Sampling with a seed is the pattern you know from `java.util.Random`. In Java you
write `new Random(0)` and every run replays the same sequence; in NumPy you write
`np.random.default_rng(0)` and get the same guarantee — a seeded generator object
you ask for draws. The difference is that `default_rng` hands you a whole array in
one call (`rng.normal(size=1000)`) instead of looping `nextGaussian()` a thousand
times, and it uses a modern, higher-quality generator than the legacy linear
congruential one behind `java.util.Random`.

A **histogram** is a `Map<Bucket, Integer>` you have built by hand many times: walk
the values, decide which bucket each falls in, increment that bucket's count. NumPy
and Matplotlib do the bucketing and counting for you, but the mental model is the
same — counts per bucket.

One reason the **normal** distribution shows up everywhere is the *central limit
idea*: add up (or average) many small independent random effects and the total
tends toward a bell shape, almost regardless of what each individual effect looked
like. Sensor noise, rounding errors, the sum of many tiny influences — they pile up
into a normal. It is worth filing away now; it explains why the bell reappears
across the rest of this course.
:::

---

## Let's build it (worked example)

Let's make a bell appear from nothing but random draws. We seed a generator for
reproducibility, pull 1000 samples from a normal distribution with `rng.normal`, and
drop them into a histogram. Watch the middle bins tower over the edges.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "1000 samples from a normal distribution fall into a bell-shaped histogram"
#| fig-alt: "A histogram of 1000 normal samples: short bars at the far-left and far-right edges, growing taller toward the centre near zero where the tallest bars stand, forming a symmetric bell shape."
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)        # fixed seed → the same draws every run
samples = rng.normal(size=1000)       # 1000 draws from the standard normal

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(samples, bins=30, color="tab:blue", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("value")
ax.set_ylabel("count (samples per bin)")
ax.set_title("1000 normal samples → a bell")
plt.show()

print("mean:", round(float(samples.mean()), 3))
print("most extreme draws:", round(float(samples.min()), 2),
      "to", round(float(samples.max()), 2))
```

The takeaway is the silhouette of the bars: the tallest bins sit near `0`, the
middle of the distribution, and the bars shrink smoothly toward both edges, where
values are rare. The printed mean lands close to `0` even though no single draw was
asked to, and the most extreme draws sit a few units out on each side — the thin
tails. That bell is the normal distribution made visible, built from samples alone.
Re-run it and, because the seed is fixed, you get the very same bell.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **distribution** is the shape that randomness takes — which values are common
   and which are rare across all possible outcomes.
2. A **uniform** distribution makes every outcome equally likely (a flat shape, like
   a fair die); a **normal** distribution clusters values near the middle and thins
   at the edges (a bell).
3. You **see** a distribution by drawing many samples and plotting a **histogram** —
   counts per bin — and the shape sharpens as samples grow.
4. **Fix the random seed** (`np.random.default_rng(0)`) so the draws, the plot, and
   the numbers are reproducible.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

A histogram bar's height is a count, but to compare bins you often want a
**fraction**: what share of all samples landed in this bin? Complete
`relative_frequency(count, total)` so it divides the bin's count by the total number
of samples. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_relfreq
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_relfreq
def relative_frequency(count, total):
    """The fraction of samples in a bin: its count divided by the total."""
    return ____  # TODO: count divided by total

run_tests([
    ("quarter of the samples", relative_frequency(250, 1000), 0.25),
    ("half", relative_frequency(1, 2), 0.5),
    ("empty bin", relative_frequency(0, 5), 0.0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

A fraction is one division: the part over the whole, `count / total`. With 250 of
1000 samples in a bin, that is `250 / 1000 = 0.25`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def relative_frequency(count, total):
    return count / total
```

`count / total` turns a raw bar height into a share of the whole. A bin holding 250
of 1000 samples has relative frequency `0.25`; an empty bin (`0` count) has `0.0`.
These fractions are what let you compare histograms with different sample sizes.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

To build a histogram bar by hand, you count how many values land inside a bin's
range. Write `in_range(values, low, high)` that returns how many entries of
`values` fall within `[low, high]` **inclusive** of both ends. Use a loop or a sum
with a condition. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_inrange
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_inrange
def in_range(values, low, high):
    """Count how many values fall within [low, high], including both ends."""
    count = 0
    for v in values:
        if ____:  # TODO: v is at least low AND at most high
            count += 1
    return count

run_tests([
    ("middle three", in_range([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 2, 4), 3),
    ("none in range", in_range([1, 2, 3], 5, 9), 0),
    ("all on the edge", in_range([0, 0, 0], 0, 0), 3),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

A value is inside the bin when it is **at least** `low` and **at most** `high`.
Python lets you chain that into one comparison: `low <= v <= high`. The `<=` on both
sides makes the ends inclusive.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def in_range(values, low, high):
    count = 0
    for v in values:
        if low <= v <= high:
            count += 1
    return count
```

`low <= v <= high` is true exactly when `v` sits inside the bin, ends included. For
`[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` with `low=2, high=4` it counts `2, 3, 4` → `3`. When the range is
a single point like `[0, 0]`, every `0` still counts because both `<=` are
inclusive, so `[0, 0, 0]` gives `3`. (The same logic, vectorised, is what
`np.histogram` runs for every bin at once.)
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Draw many samples from a
**uniform** distribution with a fixed seed (`rng = np.random.default_rng(0)`, then
`rng.uniform(size=...)` or `rng.integers(1, 7, size=...)` for a die), plot a
histogram, and describe in words how its flat shape differs from the normal's bell
you saw in the worked example.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Seeds a generator and draws a sizable sample (say 1000+) from a uniform
  distribution, then plots a histogram with `plt.hist`.
- Notes that the bars are **roughly level** across the range — every value about
  equally likely — instead of peaking in the middle.
- Contrasts this flat shape with the normal's bell, where the middle bins tower over
  the thin tails.

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)          # fixed seed → reproducible draws
samples = rng.uniform(size=2000)        # 2000 draws, each equally likely in [0, 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(samples, bins=20, color="tab:orange", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("value")
ax.set_ylabel("count (samples per bin)")
ax.set_title("2000 uniform samples → a flat shape")
plt.show()

print("min:", round(float(samples.min()), 3), "max:", round(float(samples.max()), 3))
```

The bars come out **roughly level** across `[0, 1)`: with every value equally
likely, no bin is favoured, so the histogram is a flat plateau (jagged only from
sampling noise, which smooths out as samples grow). That is the visual opposite of
the normal's bell, where the central bins tower over the thin tails. Same plotting
move, a different underlying shape — which is the point of a distribution.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch10.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now name the shape of randomness and reveal it by sampling. But a picture is
hard to compare or compute with — you want a few **numbers** that summarise a
distribution: where its centre sits and how spread out it is. Next we meet exactly
those — **mean, variance & standard deviation** (Ch11) — the handful of statistics
that turn a whole bell into three tidy numbers.
